---
title: "Background"
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

## Antimicrobial Resistance — A Global Crisis

Bacterial antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the most pressing
public health threats of the 21st century. AMR arises when bacteria
evolve mechanisms to resist the effects of antibiotics, rendering
standard treatments ineffective and enabling infections to persist and
spread.

Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing is a major driver of AMR.
Antibiotics are prescribed when not needed, or the wrong antibiotic is
chosen for the right indication, accelerating the selection of resistant
bacterial strains.

## The Burden in Primary Care

More than **80% of all antibiotic prescribing** occurs in primary care
settings. A systematic review found that antibiotics are prescribed in
approximately **52% of primary care consultations** in low- and
middle-income countries (LMICs) — a rate far exceeding clinical need.

The consequences are stark: higher morbidity, higher mortality, longer
illnesses, and increased healthcare costs — disproportionately affecting
populations in LMICs who already face constrained access to health
services.

## The Situation in Tanzania and Zanzibar

Tanzania, including the semi-autonomous Zanzibar archipelago, faces a
significant burden of AMR. Primary care dispensaries in Zanzibar
serve as the first point of contact for the majority of the population
and are a critical setting in which to improve antibiotic prescribing
practices.

Several factors make rational antibiotic prescribing difficult in this
context:

- **Clinical uncertainty:** Many acute illnesses present with overlapping
  symptoms, making it hard to distinguish bacterial from viral infections
  without laboratory support.
- **Patient expectations:** Patients often expect an antibiotic
  prescription, and clinicians may feel pressure to prescribe.
- **Longstanding prescribing culture:** Widespread antibiotic prescribing
  has become normalized in many primary care settings.
- **Limited decision-support tools:** Few digital tools are designed for
  managing adult and paediatric patients in sub-Saharan Africa, and
  those that exist are rarely integrated into the day-to-day clinical
  workflow.

## The Opportunity

The Zanzibar Ministry of Health has developed and deployed the
**Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record (ZanEMR)** system across primary
care dispensaries on Unguja island. This digital infrastructure creates
a unique opportunity to embed antibiotic stewardship features directly
into the clinician's workflow — at the point of care, without
duplicating data entry.

The DAWA project was designed to test whether combining this digital
clinical decision support with structured training and peer education can
meaningfully reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing at the primary
care level in Zanzibar.
